content.problemStatement:
Given a binary string, f(S)f(S)f(S) is defined as follows:


A prefix S[1,i]S[1, i]S[1,i] is called good if it contains at least as many ones as zeroes.
f(S)f(S)f(S) is the number of good prefixes of the string SSS.
You are given a binary string SSS of length NNN.

Your task is to maximize f(S)f(S)f(S) by performing adjacent swaps only. What is the maximum possible f(S)f(S)f(S) using any number of adjacent swaps, and what is the minimum number of swaps needed to obtain this maximum?

An adjacent swap is defined as choosing an index iii (1≤i<N1 \le i < N1≤i<N) and then swap SiS_iSi​ and Si+1S_{i + 1}Si+1​.


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of multiple lines of input.

The first line contains an integer NNN.
The second line contains a binary string SSS of length NNN.




Output Format
For each test case, output on a new line 222 integers, the maximum f(S)f(S)f(S) and the minimum swaps to obtain this maxima.
constraints: The sum of NNN over all test cases does not exceed 2⋅1052 \cdot 10^52⋅105.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test Case 1 : There are no possible swaps that change f(S)f(S)f(S).
Test Case 2 : We can convert SSS to 100100100 which has f(S)=2f(S) = 2f(S)=2 using 222 adjacent swaps (first swap S2S_2S2​ and S3S_3S3​, then S1S_1S1​ and S2S_2S2​).
